article_title: I didn't know my homelab was broken until I found split DNS
core_thesis: The article argues that split DNS is an elegant, low-effort solution for homelab users who need seamless access to self-hosted services both on their local network and remotely. The author describes how using local IPs internally and Cloudflare Tunnel externally created a fragmented experience, and how implementing split DNS via Pi-hole unified access under a single domain without changing the existing infrastructure.
detailed_summary: The author ran a homelab with 15+ services (Jellyfin, Immich, Nextcloud) on an old PC. Internally, services were accessed via local IPs (e.g., 192.168.0.115:8096). Externally, because the ISP uses CGNAT, port forwarding was impossible, so the author deployed Cloudflare Tunnel via Cloudflared Docker container. This created a dual-access method: local IP on home network, public domain via Cloudflare outside. Managing bookmarks for both became cumbersome. After researching, the author discovered split DNS (split-horizon DNS), which returns different IPs for the same domain depending on the requester's network. Using Pi-hole as the local DNS server, the author added local DNS records mapping each service's domain to the server's local IP. On the local network, Pi-hole resolves the domain to the local IP; outside, public DNS resolves to Cloudflare's IP. This required configuring all devices to use Pi-hole as DNS (already done via Omada Controller). The author also faced IPv6 issues because Cloudflare exposes both IPv4 and IPv6, but Pi-hole only forwarded IPv4; the temporary fix was disabling IPv6 on client devices. The setup made access seamless: the same domain works everywhere, and local traffic no longer routes through the internet. The article concludes that split DNS complemented the existing setup without replacing it.

entities.name: Shekhar Vaidya
entities.role: Author and homelab enthusiast who implemented split DNS

entities.name: Pi-hole
entities.role: Network-wide DNS server used to implement local DNS records for split DNS

entities.name: Cloudflare Tunnel
entities.role: Used to expose homelab services to the internet via a public domain

entities.name: Cloudflared
entities.role: Docker container that connects the homelab to Cloudflare Tunnel

entities.name: CGNAT
entities.role: ISP-imposed Carrier-Grade NAT that prevented port forwarding

entities.name: Omada Controller
entities.role: Network management controller used to set Pi-hole as the DNS server on all devices

key_data_points: The author self-hosts over 15 services (Jellyfin, Immich, Nextcloud) on an old PC, accessed via local IPs internally and Cloudflare Tunnel externally. (🟢 Relevant to homelab: shows scale of typical homelab and the fragmentation problem.)

ISP uses CGNAT, making port forwarding impossible, forcing reliance on Cloudflare Tunnel for external access. (🟢 Relevant to homelab networking: common obstacle; split DNS bypasses the need for port forwarding.)

Split DNS uses Pi-hole to return the server's local IP for domains when the request originates from the local network, while public DNS returns Cloudflare's IP for external requests. (🟢 Relevant to homelab: a clean, low-overhead solution that doesn't require changing existing services.)

The author encountered IPv6 issues because Cloudflare exposes both IPv4 and IPv6, but Pi-hole only forwarded IPv4; temporary fix was disabling IPv6 on client devices. (🟢 Relevant to homelab: highlights a common pitfall and a workaround.)
The setup required all devices to use Pi-hole as DNS, which was already configured via Omada Controller. (🟢 Relevant to homelab: emphasizes the importance of centralized DNS management.)
methodology_steps: Identify the problem: services accessible via local IP on home network and via Cloudflare Tunnel externally, requiring separate bookmarks.
Deploy Pi-hole as a network-wide DNS server (already in use).
Add local DNS records in Pi-hole: for each service domain (e.g., jellyfin.yourdomain.com), set the A record to the server's local IP address (e.g., 192.168.0.115).
Ensure all devices on the local network use Pi-hole as their DNS server (configured via router/DHCP or Omada Controller).
Keep Cloudflare Tunnel running for external access; no changes needed on the Cloudflare side.
Test by accessing the same domain from inside and outside the network: internally it resolves to local IP, externally to Cloudflare IP.
Handle IPv6: if Cloudflare exposes IPv6 and Pi-hole only handles IPv4, disable IPv6 on client devices as a temporary workaround, or configure Pi-hole to also handle IPv6 records.
conclusions: Split DNS elegantly solves the dual-access problem in homelabs by providing different DNS responses based on the requester's network, without altering the existing service setup. The author found that the solution was already available (Pi-hole) and required only adding local DNS records.

The fix is simple and complements existing infrastructure (Cloudflare Tunnel, Docker, Pi-hole) rather than replacing it. It eliminates the need for separate bookmarks and makes local traffic stay local, improving performance.

IPv6 can be a stumbling block; the author's temporary fix (disabling IPv6) is not ideal, but the article notes that Pi-hole does support IPv6 static entries, suggesting a proper configuration is possible.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to your homelab interests as it provides a practical, low-effort solution to a common networking pain point: seamless access to self-hosted services both locally and remotely. As a programmer and homelab enthusiast, you can directly apply split DNS using Pi-hole (or Technitium DNS) to unify access to your services, eliminating the need for separate bookmarks or VPNs. The article also touches on IPv6 issues, which you may encounter, and offers a workaround. The comments suggest alternative approaches (reverse proxy, Tailscale) that you can evaluate for your own setup. This is a quick win that improves daily usability.
extra_advice: Consider using a reverse proxy (like Nginx Proxy Manager or Caddy) in front of your services to handle port numbers and SSL termination. Combined with split DNS, you can access all services via standard ports (80/443) without remembering port numbers. For external access, you can pair split DNS with a VPN (WireGuard/Tailscale) instead of Cloudflare Tunnel if you prefer not to expose services to the public internet. Also, properly configure Pi-hole's IPv6 support to avoid disabling IPv6 on clients.
